chunk_summary: This section explores context assessment and strategy selection in implementation research for nutrition practice, emphasizing frameworks like CFIR and RE-AIM to identify barriers and facilitators, and the ERIC taxonomy for actionable strategies tailored to clinical settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of implementation research in translating nutrition interventions into practice.
Learn to assess context using frameworks like CFIR and RE-AIM for measurable outcomes.
Identify and select appropriate implementation strategies from the ERIC taxonomy.
Apply practical strategies to reduce clinician cognitive load in high-pressure settings.
key_facts: CFIR assesses context across five domains: intervention characteristics, inner setting, outer setting, individual characteristics, and process.
RE-AIM evaluates interventions across five dimensions: reach, effectiveness, adoption, implementation, and maintenance.
ERIC taxonomy includes strategies like workflow redesign, task shifting, and patient-facing materials.
Example implementation target: Deliver a 60-second sugar-sweetened beverage reduction script to adolescents with overweight.
In high-volume clinics, strategies like integrating prompts into case sheets or pre-printing handouts in local languages can reduce cognitive load.
reference_quote: CFIR structures assessment across intervention characteristics, inner setting, outer setting, individual characteristics, and process, helping you identify barriers and facilitators that are actually modifiable. — CFIR Guide
document_key_topics: Practical strategies for high-pressure clinical settings
